nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reefs at depths of approximately 5–60 m

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on fish and large crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, has been reported to accept fresh or frozen fish flesh, squid, octopus, and shrimp; live feeder fish may be needed initially to trigger a feeding response. Feeding with tongs is strongly advised due to poor vision and risk of accidental bites. Generally fed every 2–4 days to satiation.

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed

summary: A large, strikingly patterned moray eel from Indo-Pacific reefs, generally considered suitable for experienced saltwater keepers who can provide a spacious, well-secured aquarium with ample rockwork for hiding.
careNotes:
The Japanese Dragon Eel (Enchelycore pardalis) is one of the most visually striking moray eels available in the saltwater hobby, recognised by its mottled orange, black, and white body, elaborate nostril flares, and pronounced supra-orbital horns along the top of the head. Specimens have been reported to reach up to 92 cm (around 36 inches) in well-maintained aquariums, though most captive individuals tend to remain in the 60–75 cm range.

A tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential; this species has been known to escape through any small gap. All rockwork should be secured with aquarium-safe adhesive or cable ties, as this eel tends to rearrange the aquascape when moving through caves.

The Japanese Dragon Eel is nocturnal by nature and may hide for extended periods while acclimating — periods of refusing food for several weeks after introduction have been reported. Once settled, it generally becomes more active and will often rest with its head visible at a cave entrance. Feeding tongs are strongly recommended to avoid accidental bites; its eyesight is poor and it relies largely on scent, which can lead to unintended strikes at hands near the tank.

Although coral tissue is not typically targeted, the eel's large size and powerful movement can dislodge or damage coral placements. It will consume any crustacean within reach, making it incompatible with cleaner shrimp, hermit crabs, and most cleanup crew species.

Captive lifespan has been reported at around 10 years in well-kept aquariums, while wild specimens are thought to live up to 20 years. The species is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.
